questiontext.text: Which of the following are valid metrics on \(\mathbb{R}^n\)? Select ALL correct options.

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (A) and (C)

(A) TRUE: The Euclidean metric \(d_2(x,y)=\sqrt{\sum(x_i-y_i)^2}\) is a metric (triangle inequality from Cauchy-Schwarz). (C) TRUE: The sup metric \(d_\infty(x,y)=\max_i|x_i-y_i|\) is a metric (easy to verify). (B) FALSE: \(d(x,y)=(\sum|x_i-y_i|^{1/2})^2\) is NOT a metric for \(n\geq2\) (triangle inequality fails). (D) FALSE: \(d(x,y)=|x_1-y_1|\cdot|x_2-y_2|\) is not a metric (not positive definite for distinct points with same first coordinate).

answer.text: Euclidean metric: \(d_2(x,y) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^n(x_i-y_i)^2}\)
Sup metric: \(d_\infty(x,y) = \max_{1\leq i\leq n}|x_i - y_i|\)

name.text: Metric Spaces - Open and Closed Sets

questiontext.text: In a metric space \((X,d)\), select ALL correct statements:

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (A) and (D)

(A) TRUE: arbitrary unions of open sets are open (by definition of topology). (D) TRUE: finite intersections of open sets are open (not infinite — e.g., \(\bigcap_{n=1}^\infty(-1/n,1/n)=\{0\}\) is closed). (B) FALSE: infinite intersections of open sets need not be open (as shown). (C) FALSE: finite unions of closed sets are closed, not arbitrary (e.g., \(\bigcup_{n=1}^\infty[1/n,1]=[0,1]\setminus\{0\}\cup\{0\}...\) actually \(=[0,1]\) — better: arbitrary union of closed sets need not be closed: \(\bigcup_{n=2}^\infty[1/n,1]=(0,1]\) open).

answer.text: Arbitrary unions of open sets are open
Arbitrary intersections of open sets are open
Arbitrary unions of closed sets are closed
Finite intersections of open sets are open

questiontext.text: Which of the following metric spaces are COMPLETE? Select ALL correct options.

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (A) and (D)

(A) TRUE: \(\mathbb{R}^n\) with Euclidean metric is complete (Cauchy sequences converge). (D) TRUE: \(C[a,b]\) with sup norm \(\|f\|_\infty = \sup|f(x)|\) is a complete metric space (Banach space). (B) FALSE: \(\mathbb{Q}\) with the usual metric is NOT complete (e.g., \(1, 1.4, 1.41, \ldots \to \sqrt{2}\notin\mathbb{Q}\)). (C) FALSE: \((0,1)\) with the usual metric is NOT complete (e.g., \(1/n\) is Cauchy but converges to \(0\notin(0,1)\)).

answer.text: \(\mathbb{R}^n\) with the Euclidean metric
\(\mathbb{Q}\) with the usual metric \(d(x,y)=|x-y|\)
\((0,1)\) with the usual metric
\(C[a,b]\) with the sup norm metric \(d(f,g)=\|f-g\|_\infty\)

questiontext.text: In a metric space \((X,d)\), a subset \(K\) is compact if and only if. Select ALL equivalent conditions.

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (B) and (C)

In a metric space, the following are equivalent: (B) Sequential compactness: every sequence in \(K\) has a subsequence converging in \(K\). (C) Complete + Totally bounded: \(K\) can be covered by finitely many \(\epsilon\)-balls for every \(\epsilon>0\), and every Cauchy sequence converges. (A) FALSE: closed and bounded is equivalent to compact ONLY in \(\mathbb{R}^n\) (Heine-Borel), not in general metric spaces. (D) FALSE: separability (countable dense subset) is neither necessary nor sufficient for compactness.

answer.text: Closed and bounded (valid in ALL metric spaces)
Every sequence in \(K\) has a subsequence converging to a point in \(K\) (sequentially compact)
Complete and totally bounded
Separable (has a countable dense subset)

name.text: Metric Spaces - Banach Fixed Point Theorem

questiontext.text: The Banach Fixed Point (Contraction Mapping) Theorem states: if \((X,d)\) is a complete metric space and \(T:X\to X\) is a contraction (\(\exists\,k<1: d(Tx,Ty)\leq k\,d(x,y)\) for all \(x,y\)), then select ALL correct conclusions:

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (A) and (D)

(A) TRUE: a contraction on a complete metric space has a unique fixed point. (D) TRUE: successive approximations \(x_n = T^n(x_0)\) converge to the fixed point at rate \(k^n d(x_0,Tx_0)/(1-k)\). (B) FALSE: the fixed point is UNIQUE. (C) FALSE: completeness of \(X\) is essential — on an incomplete space, a contraction may have no fixed point (e.g., \(T(x)=x/2\) on \((0,1)\) is a contraction without fixed point).

answer.text: \(T\) has a unique fixed point in \(X\)
\(T\) may have infinitely many fixed points
Completeness is not necessary; any metric space works
Picard iterations \(x_{n+1}=T(x_n)\) converge to the fixed point for any initial \(x_0\)

questiontext.text: The Baire Category Theorem states that a complete metric space is of the second category (not meager). Select ALL correct consequences:

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (B) and (C)

(B) TRUE: a complete metric space cannot be expressed as a countable union of nowhere dense closed sets (BCT). (C) TRUE: if a complete metric space is a countable union of closed sets \(F_n\), then at least one \(F_n\) has non-empty interior — a direct consequence of BCT. (A) FALSE: \(\mathbb{R}\) (complete) is the countable union of singletons \(\{r\}\), which are closed BUT are nowhere dense; \(\mathbb{R}\) itself is NOT a countable union of nowhere dense sets — consistent with BCT. (D) FALSE: BCT does not imply uncountability directly.

answer.text: \(\mathbb{R}\) cannot be written as a countable union of closed sets
A complete metric space cannot be a countable union of nowhere dense sets
If \(X = \bigcup_{n=1}^\infty F_n\) (closed), then at least one \(F_n\) has non-empty interior
Every complete metric space is uncountable

questiontext.text: A metric space \((X,d)\) is totally bounded if for every \(\epsilon>0\), \(X\) can be covered by finitely many \(\epsilon\)-balls. Select ALL correct statements:

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (A) and (C)

(A) TRUE: compact implies totally bounded (a compact space can be covered by finitely many open sets, in particular finitely many \(\epsilon\)-balls). (C) TRUE: totally bounded implies bounded (since finitely many \(\epsilon\)-balls cover \(X\), the diameter is bounded). (B) FALSE: bounded does NOT imply totally bounded (e.g., the unit ball in an infinite-dimensional Banach space is bounded but not totally bounded). (D) FALSE: totally bounded + complete = compact, not totally bounded = compact by itself.

answer.text: Compact metric spaces are totally bounded
Bounded metric spaces are always totally bounded
Totally bounded spaces are bounded
Totally bounded always implies compact

name.text: Metric Spaces - Uniform Continuity on Compact Sets

questiontext.text: Let \(f: (X,d_X) \to (Y,d_Y)\) be a continuous map where \(X\) is compact. Select ALL correct statements:

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (B) and (D)

(B) TRUE: continuous maps on compact metric spaces are uniformly continuous (Heine-Cantor theorem, generalized to metric spaces). (D) TRUE: the image \(f(X)\) of a compact set under a continuous map is compact. (A) FALSE: \(f\) need not be differentiable. (C) FALSE: open subsets of \(X\) need not map to open sets; continuous maps in general are not open maps.

answer.text: \(f\) is differentiable on \(X\)
\(f\) is uniformly continuous on \(X\)
\(f\) maps open sets to open sets (open map)
\(f(X)\) is compact in \(Y\)

questiontext.text: Two metrics \(d_1\) and \(d_2\) on the same set \(X\) are equivalent (topologically) if they generate the same topology. Select ALL correct implications of equivalent metrics:

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (A) and (C)

(A) TRUE: equivalent metrics have the same open sets, hence the same convergent sequences. (C) TRUE: same open sets means same notions of continuity — a function is continuous w.r.t. \(d_1\) iff it is w.r.t. \(d_2\). (B) FALSE: equivalent metrics need NOT be uniformly equivalent (uniform equivalence requires \(c_1 d_1\leq d_2\leq c_2 d_1\)). E.g., \(d(x,y)=|x-y|\) and \(d'(x,y)=|x-y|/(1+|x-y|)\) on \(\mathbb{R}\) are equivalent but not uniformly equivalent. (D) FALSE: completeness is NOT a topological property and may differ for equivalent metrics.

answer.text: A sequence converges in \((X,d_1)\) iff it converges in \((X,d_2)\)
Uniform continuity is the same in both metrics
Continuity of maps to/from \(X\) is the same under both metrics
Completeness is always preserved under equivalent metrics

name.text: Metric Spaces - Completion of a Metric Space

questiontext.text: Every metric space \((X,d)\) has a completion \((\hat{X},\hat{d})\). Select ALL correct statements about the completion:

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (B) and (D)

(B) TRUE: \(X\) is isometrically embedded as a dense subspace of its completion \(\hat{X}\). (D) TRUE: the completion is unique up to isometry — any two completions of \((X,d)\) are isometrically isomorphic (fixing elements of \(X\)). (A) FALSE: the completion may not be bounded (e.g., completion of \(\mathbb{Q}\) is \(\mathbb{R}\), which is unbounded). (C) FALSE: if \(X\) is already complete, \(\hat{X}=X\) — completion adds only limit points of Cauchy sequences not already in \(X\).

answer.text: The completion is always bounded
\(X\) embeds isometrically as a dense subspace of \(\hat{X}\)
The completion \(\hat{X}\) is always strictly larger than \(X\)
The completion is unique up to isometry

name.text: Metric Spaces - Connectedness in Metric Spaces

questiontext.text: Which of the following metric spaces are connected? Select ALL correct options.

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (A) and (D)

(A) TRUE: \(\mathbb{R}^n\) is connected for all \(n\geq1\) (it is path-connected). (D) TRUE: any interval \((a,b)\) in \(\mathbb{R}\) is connected (connected subsets of \(\mathbb{R}\) are exactly the intervals). (B) FALSE: \(\mathbb{Q}\) is NOT connected — for any irrational \(c\), \(\mathbb{Q} = (\mathbb{Q}\cap(-\infty,c))\cup(\mathbb{Q}\cap(c,\infty))\) is a disconnection. (C) FALSE: \(\{0,1\}\) with discrete metric is disconnected (both points are isolated open sets).

answer.text: \(\mathbb{R}^n\) with the Euclidean metric
\(\mathbb{Q}\) with the usual metric
\(\{0,1\}\) with the discrete metric
Any open interval \((a,b) \subset \mathbb{R}\)

questiontext.text: In the discrete metric space \((X, d)\) where \(d(x,y)=1\) for \(x\neq y\) and \(d(x,x)=0\). Select ALL correct statements:

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (B) and (C)

(B) TRUE: every subset is both open and closed (clopen) in the discrete metric — every singleton \(\{x\} = B(x,1/2)\) is open. (C) TRUE: every function from a discrete metric space is continuous (preimage of any set is a subset of \(X\), hence open in the discrete topology). (A) FALSE: a convergent sequence in the discrete metric must be eventually constant (since for convergence, \(d(x_n,L)<1/2\) eventually means \(x_n=L\)). (D) FALSE: \(X\) is compact under discrete metric iff \(X\) is FINITE (otherwise the open cover of singletons has no finite subcover).

answer.text: Every sequence in \(X\) converges
Every subset of \(X\) is both open and closed
Every function from \((X,d)\) to any metric space is continuous
If \(X\) is infinite, \((X,d)\) is compact

name.text: Metric Spaces - Open Mapping and Homeomorphism

questiontext.text: A continuous bijection \(f: X \to Y\) between metric spaces. Select ALL correct statements:

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (C) and (D)

(C) TRUE: if additionally \(X\) is compact and \(Y\) is Hausdorff (e.g., both metric spaces), then a continuous bijection is a homeomorphism. (D) TRUE: by compactness, \(f^{-1}\) is automatically continuous. (A) FALSE: a continuous bijection need not be a homeomorphism in general — e.g., the map \([0,2\pi) \to S^1\), \(t\mapsto e^{it}\) is a continuous bijection but NOT a homeomorphism (the inverse is not continuous at \(e^{i\cdot0}=1\)). (B) FALSE: \(f\) may not be an isometry.

answer.text: Must be a homeomorphism (continuous inverse)
Must be an isometry
Is a homeomorphism if \(X\) is compact and \(Y\) is a metric space (hence Hausdorff)
Has a continuous inverse when \(X\) is compact

name.text: Metric Spaces - Cauchy Sequences and Convergence

questiontext.text: Regarding Cauchy sequences in metric spaces. Select ALL correct statements:

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (A) and (D)

(A) TRUE: every convergent sequence is Cauchy (triangle inequality: \(d(x_m,x_n)\leq d(x_m,L)+d(L,x_n)\to0\)). (D) TRUE: every Cauchy sequence is bounded (from some \(N\) onward, all terms within distance 1 of \(x_N\), so the whole sequence is bounded). (B) FALSE: Cauchy sequences need not converge in incomplete spaces (e.g., in \(\mathbb{Q}\)). (C) FALSE: bounded sequences need not be Cauchy (e.g., \((-1)^n\) is bounded but not Cauchy in \(\mathbb{R}\)).

answer.text: Every convergent sequence is a Cauchy sequence
Every Cauchy sequence converges (in any metric space)
Every bounded sequence is Cauchy
Every Cauchy sequence is bounded

questiontext.text: By the Heine-Borel theorem, in \(\mathbb{R}^n\) a set \(K\) is compact if and only if. Select ALL correct equivalent conditions (in \(\mathbb{R}^n\)):

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (B) and (C)

In \(\mathbb{R}^n\): Heine-Borel: compact \(\iff\) closed and bounded. (B) TRUE: closed and bounded is precisely the Heine-Borel characterization of compactness in \(\mathbb{R}^n\). (C) TRUE: sequentially compact (every sequence has convergent subsequence in \(K\)) is equivalent to compact in metric spaces. (A) FALSE: open and bounded (e.g., open ball) is NOT compact. (D) FALSE: complete alone is not sufficient (e.g., \(\mathbb{R}^n\) is complete but not compact).

answer.text: Open and bounded
Closed and bounded
Every sequence in \(K\) has a subsequence converging in \(K\)
Complete (every Cauchy sequence converges in \(K\))

questiontext.text: Given metric spaces \((X_1,d_1)\) and \((X_2,d_2)\), which of the following are valid metrics on \(X_1\times X_2\)? Select ALL correct options.

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (A) and (C)

(A) TRUE: \(d((x_1,x_2),(y_1,y_2)) = \max(d_1(x_1,y_1), d_2(x_2,y_2))\) is a metric (sup metric on product). (C) TRUE: \(d_1(x_1,y_1)+d_2(x_2,y_2)\) is also a valid metric. Both generate the same product topology. (B) FALSE: \(d_1(x_1,y_1)\cdot d_2(x_2,y_2)\) is NOT a metric (e.g., if \(x_1=y_1\) and \(x_2\neq y_2\), distance is 0 despite \((x_1,x_2)\neq(y_1,y_2)\)). (D) FALSE: \(|d_1-d_2|\) is not a metric on the product space.

questiontext.text: A subset \(D\) of a metric space \((X,d)\) is dense if \(\overline{D} = X\). Select ALL equivalent conditions:

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (B) and (C)

(B) TRUE: \(D\) is dense iff every open ball \(B(x,\epsilon)\) meets \(D\) (i.e., every non-empty open set intersects \(D\)). (C) TRUE: \(D\) is dense iff every point of \(X\) is a limit point of \(D\) (or in \(D\)) — equivalently, every \(x\in X\) can be approximated by elements of \(D\). (A) FALSE: \(D\) dense does NOT require \(D = X\) (e.g., \(\mathbb{Q}\) is dense in \(\mathbb{R}\)). (D) FALSE: dense does not mean \(D\) is open.

answer.text: \(D = X\) (the whole space)
Every non-empty open set in \(X\) intersects \(D\)
For every \(x\in X\) and \(\epsilon>0\), there exists \(d\in D\) with \(\rho(x,d)<\epsilon\)
\(D\) is an open subset of \(X\)

name.text: Metric Spaces - Uniform Continuity and Cauchy Sequences

questiontext.text: Let \(f: (X,d_X) \to (Y,d_Y)\) be uniformly continuous. Select ALL correct statements:

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (A) and (C)

(A) TRUE: uniformly continuous maps preserve Cauchy sequences — if \(\{x_n\}\) is Cauchy in \(X\), then \(\{f(x_n)\}\) is Cauchy in \(Y\). This follows from the definition: given \(\epsilon>0\), find \(\delta>0\) such that \(d_X(x,y)<\delta\Rightarrow d_Y(f(x),f(y))<\epsilon\); since \(\{x_n\}\) is Cauchy, eventually \(d_X(x_m,x_n)<\delta\), so \(d_Y(f(x_m),f(x_n))<\epsilon\). (C) TRUE: consequence — uniform continuity extends to the completion. (B) FALSE: merely continuous maps do NOT necessarily preserve Cauchy sequences. (D) FALSE: uniform continuity alone doesn't require compactness.

answer.text: If \(\{x_n\}\) is a Cauchy sequence in \(X\), then \(\{f(x_n)\}\) is Cauchy in \(Y\)
Merely continuous maps also preserve Cauchy sequences
\(f\) extends uniquely to a uniformly continuous map \(\hat{f}: \hat{X} \to \hat{Y}\) (if \(Y\) is complete)
\(X\) must be compact for \(f\) to be uniformly continuous

name.text: Metric Spaces - Nowhere Dense and Interior

questiontext.text: A set \(E\) in a metric space is nowhere dense if its closure \(\overline{E}\) has empty interior. Select ALL correct statements:

generalfeedback.text:
Answers: (B) and (D)

(B) TRUE: the complement of a nowhere dense set's closure is a dense open set: \(X\setminus\overline{E}\) is open and dense. (D) TRUE: \(\mathbb{Z}\) in \(\mathbb{R}\) is closed (hence \(\overline{\mathbb{Z}}=\mathbb{Z}\)) and has empty interior (no open interval fits in \(\mathbb{Z}\)), so it is nowhere dense. (A) FALSE: \(\mathbb{Q}\) in \(\mathbb{R}\) is NOT nowhere dense — its closure is \(\mathbb{R}\), which has non-empty interior. (C) FALSE: a nowhere dense set need not be finite — e.g., Cantor set is uncountable and nowhere dense.

answer.text: \(\mathbb{Q}\) is nowhere dense in \(\mathbb{R}\)
The complement of \(\overline{E}\) is a dense open set when \(E\) is nowhere dense
Every nowhere dense set is finite
\(\mathbb{Z}\) is nowhere dense in \(\mathbb{R}\)
